title: Multi-Deck Mixing Concepts
description: Think beyond two decks — use one deck for loops, one for the main track, and manage transitions between all sources.

steps.instruction: Watch: Deck A plays the main track. Deck B is set to a 2-bar loop — a percussion break that layers on top.
Watch: Deck B's loop plays at 50% volume beneath Deck A — an accent, not a replacement. Then it fades.
Watch: Deck A transitions to a new track. Deck B was the bridge — it kept the energy up during the changeover.

phases.instruction: Two decks let you A-B between tracks. But what if you treat Deck B as a texture layer rather than the next song? Drop in a drum loop, an acapella, a riser — while Deck A keeps the main track going. Then when the moment is right, transition Deck A to the next song. You have three elements working: main track, layer, next track.

steps.instruction: Deck A plays your main track at 80% volume. This is the anchor — the crowd is focused here.
Set up a loop on Deck B — ideally a 4-bar percussion break or a complementary element. Keep Deck B's volume at zero for now.
Beatmatch Deck B's loop to Deck A. The loop must be in perfect time or it will clash.
At a phrase boundary — bring Deck B's loop in at 40% volume. It should sit under the main track, not over it.
Let both play together for 8 bars. Listen to the layered texture. The bass from Deck A and the percussion from Deck B should lock.
Start fading Deck A. As it drops, keep Deck B's loop at 40% — the loop bridges the gap.
Fade Deck A to zero. The loop continues. Bring Deck B up to 80% — the loop is now the main track.
